title: The balance of power
gist: Resolution Foundation's weekly research roundup argues the UK's fiscal framework has been chronically too loose (the OBR has wrongly projected budget balance in 23 of 31 forecasts), while also surveying rising anti-Muslim hostility, childhood inequality's effect on sharing, a puzzle in UK mental-health statistics, Japan's rural depopulation trade-offs, and gender gaps in German inheritance.

themes.theme: UK fiscal framework and political transition

insights.claim: A new UK Prime Minister has been confirmed and cabinet appointments (including who will hold the Chancellor's role, No. 11) are still being speculated on, with the incoming team set to inherit an economic malaise and fiscal morass.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Watch for the new Chancellor appointment and early fiscal signaling as a near-term catalyst for UK gilt and sterling sentiment.

insights.claim: Since the Office for Budget Responsibility's founding, it has projected the UK government's current budget (day-to-day spending plus debt interest, less tax revenue) to reach balance by the end of the five-year forecast period in 23 of 31 fiscal events.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: A near-consistent pattern of overly optimistic OBR forecasts implies structural downside risk to any current fiscal projections used to justify UK spending or tax plans.

insights.claim: Excluding the pandemic and post-financial-crisis periods, the OBR has forecast a budget deficit at the end of a five-year window only once, yet in reality the UK government has run an actual current budget surplus only once, in 2018-19.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: The gap between OBR forecasts and outturns is a track record investors can use to discount official UK fiscal projections.

insights.claim: The core problem with the UK's fiscal framework is not that it has been too tight, but that it has been too loose, since successive governments have repeatedly spent more than they raised in tax despite forecasts of balance.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Runs counter to the popular framing of UK fiscal rules as excessively austere; positions any incoming Chancellor's room for giveaways as more constrained than headline forecasts suggest.
insights.quote: the problem with our fiscal framework is not that it has been too tight, but too loose

insights.claim: With UK government debt and borrowing costs continuing to rise, whoever leads the Treasury next week will need to make tough, realistic fiscal choices rather than voter-friendly announcements; a separate Resolution Foundation report on funding higher public investment via UK public financial institutions is due the following week.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Signals an upcoming Resolution Foundation publication on UK public investment vehicles worth tracking for policy direction on infrastructure financing.

insights.claim: A joint British Muslim Trust and British Future survey found that over half (56 per cent) of Muslims in Britain reported experiencing religious prejudice in the past year, consistent with record-breaking hate crime levels recorded in 2025.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Documents a deteriorating social climate that could feed into policy responses, community relations funding, or political risk around social cohesion in the UK.

insights.claim: Following the September 'Unite the Kingdom' demonstration, 61 per cent of British Muslims surveyed said they felt more worried about their personal safety, indicating the protests directly stoked fear within the community.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Large-scale political demonstrations are having measurable, immediate effects on minority communities' sense of safety, a signal for social-cohesion and security policy risk.

insights.claim: More than a third (37 per cent) of the British public agree that growth in the Muslim population poses a foundational threat to UK culture, according to the British Muslim Trust/British Future survey.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: A sizeable minority holding this view underscores ongoing polarization that could shape immigration and integration policy debates in the UK.
insights.quote: the growth in the Muslim population poses a foundational threat to UK culture

insights.claim: A Fairness Foundation study combining data from 1,790 participants across 7 countries and 21 research papers found that growing up in an unequal society reduces children's willingness to share.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Suggests income inequality has behavioral and social costs beyond economics, relevant to long-run social capital and cohesion arguments for redistribution.

insights.claim: The reduced willingness to share fades as children get older, but older children perceive inequality as more unfair than younger children and are more likely to believe in the need for equitable redistribution of resources.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Attitudes favoring redistribution appear to strengthen with age and exposure to inequality, a generational dynamic worth watching in future public opinion on tax and welfare policy.

insights.claim: Analysis by Ben Baumberg Geiger, picked up by John Burn-Murdoch in the FT, finds that reported psychological distress and mental illness have been rising in the UK even though significant activity limitations arising from poor mental health have stayed flat.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Challenges the assumption that rising mental-ill-health statistics reflect a proportional rise in functional impairment, which matters for how welfare and disability benefit systems are calibrated.

insights.claim: Ben Baumberg Geiger suggests the divergence between rising distress and flat activity limitations may partly reflect pressures towards medicalisation, including welfare and employment systems that require people to identify as 'sick' to access needed support, or a broader cultural shift toward viewing mental distress as a long-term condition.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: If welfare-system incentives are inflating measured mental illness rates, that has direct implications for how policymakers interpret disability and incapacity benefit caseload growth.

insights.claim: John Burn-Murdoch observes that different groups of people label the same behaviour or symptoms very differently, particularly along generational lines, with young people much more likely than older people to identify psychological distress as a symptom of a long-term condition.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: A generational shift in how symptoms are labeled could be driving apparent trends in youth mental health statistics independent of any change in underlying prevalence.
insights.quote: different groups of people label the same behaviour or symptoms very differently

insights.claim: Both Ben Baumberg Geiger and John Burn-Murdoch agree the rise in reported psychological distress is real, but Geiger cautions that more research is needed before the cause can be considered definitive.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: The causal story behind UK mental-health trend data remains unsettled, so policy or market conclusions drawn from it should be held loosely pending further research.

themes.theme: Demographic ageing and regional policy trade-offs (Japan)

insights.claim: Japan's national population aged 65 and over is projected to reach 37 per cent by 2050, but some rural municipalities already have elderly population shares approaching 50 per cent, while large cities remain younger and denser.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Illustrates how population ageing is spatially concentrated rather than uniform, a pattern relevant to any country (including the UK) weighing place-based demographic and fiscal policy.

insights.claim: New research on Japan finds younger people are moving to Tokyo to have their children rather than staying in or returning to the rural areas they came from, accelerating rural depopulation.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Urban migration of young families is a self-reinforcing driver of rural decline that place-based policy would need to directly counteract, not just subsidize.

insights.claim: Modelling in the Japan research shows a 5 per cent income transfer targeted at the oldest prefectures could nearly double their population by 2065 by slowing the hollowing-out of rural areas.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Quantifies how large a fiscal transfer would need to be to meaningfully reverse rural depopulation, a benchmark for evaluating similarly-aimed UK 'levelling up' style policies.

insights.claim: The same Japan modelling finds that income transfers large enough to nearly double rural prefecture populations by 2065 would also lower labour income per capita by more than 1 per cent and raise fiscal spending per capita by about 0.5 per cent, implying that policies aiming to raise growth in every locality ('every postcode') carry real costs as well as benefits.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: A caution against assuming place-based regional levelling-up policy is a free lunch; investors and policymakers evaluating UK regional investment programs should weight these efficiency costs.

themes.theme: Gender gap in inheritance and gifts

insights.claim: Analysis of more than four decades of German data finds women lose out on inheritance and gifts relative to men, with men receiving larger gifts and about €12,000 more in overall transfers.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Documents a persistent, multi-decade gender gap in intergenerational wealth transfers that compounds other sources of the gender wealth gap.

insights.claim: In West Germany specifically, younger women receive €22,000 less in inheritances and €31,000 less in gifts than their male counterparts.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Regional (West vs East Germany) variation in the gender transfer gap points to lingering cultural/institutional legacy effects worth isolating in any policy response.

insights.claim: The authors of the German inheritance study attribute the gender gap in transfers to persistent gendered family norms in West Germany, such as sons being viewed as custodians of familial wealth.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: Cultural norms rather than purely economic factors are identified as a driver, meaning the gap may persist even as income inequality narrows.

insights.claim: Inheritances and gifts are found to contribute 7 per cent to the overall gender wealth gap in Germany; separately, Resolution Foundation's own UK research has shown high-income young people receive larger gifts earlier in life than low-income young people, meaning both gender and income inequality compound intergenerational wealth imbalances via transfers.
insights.speaker: Resolution Foundation
insights.so_what: As inheritance and lifetime gifting play a growing role in wealth accumulation, closing gender gaps in transfers becomes a distinct policy lever separate from closing income gaps.
